one_line_takeaway: LLM jailbreak attack/defense numbers are dominated by undocumented pipeline choices (chat templates, tokenization, quantization, decoding, judge selection, tiny datasets) -- so the field must standardize datasets, control attack budgets, report multiple judges with manual verification, and move beyond greedy decoding before ASR comparisons can be trusted.
key_finding: Seemingly minor, undocumented pipeline choices swing headline safety numbers enough to invalidate cross-paper comparisons. Concretely: reasonable GCG implementation variations move ASR against Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct between 71% and 85% (and down to 47% with no system message); mishandling the whitespace token cuts GCG ASR by 28%; and different popular judges disagree by up to 25% for specific attacks and up to 24% across models. Combined with datasets of only ~100-500 prompts (often subsampled to as few as 40-50), these effects mean much reported progress may be measurement noise rather than genuine robustness gains.
core_contribution: A position/meta-science paper (ICML 2026) arguing that current LLM safety-alignment evaluations are unreliable because noise and bias are injected at every stage of the evaluation pipeline. The paper's own contribution is a systematic decomposition of that pipeline into three stages -- (1) Datasets, (2) Algorithms/optimization for automated red-teaming, (3) Evaluation/LLM judging -- with concrete diagnoses at each stage, small original case studies that quantify the impact, and a set of actionable guidelines plus a self-critiquing 'Alternative Views' section. It does NOT propose a new attack, defense, dataset, or benchmark; its deliverable is the diagnosis and the recommended best practices for making future attack/defense results comparable and reproducible.
threat_model: The paper studies the standard adversarial LLM-robustness setting (defined in its own Appendix A.1.1): an attacker issues adversarial/jailbreak prompts and 'robustness' is the model's ability to stay aligned and refuse harmful requests. Rather than proposing a threat model, the paper is a critique OF how the community measures attacks (e.g. GCG, PAIR, AutoDAN, BEAST, PGD) and defenses (e.g. R2D2, circuit breakers) under this threat model. Key meta-level point: because targets, decoding, judges and datasets are inconsistent, reported attack-success-rate (ASR) numbers are not a faithful measure of real-world robustness -- e.g. greedy decoding understates risk versus temperature-1 sampling, and many-trial attacks benefit from judge false-positive rates.
limitations: By its own framing it is a position paper: it offers no new benchmark, tool, or defended model, and several recommendations (larger datasets, multilingual/multi-turn data, consistent budgets, held-out quarterly eval sets) are acknowledged as hard or unresolved, especially for compute-constrained academic labs. The empirical case studies are illustrative and narrow (mostly GCG on one or two Llama models, two judges) rather than a comprehensive benchmark. The authors themselves present an 'Alternative Views' section conceding that small datasets have real practical value, that flawed judges may be unavoidable given language's ambiguity, and that ML fields have historically progressed despite noisy metrics -- somewhat tempering the strength of the central claim. Recommendations depend on community/reviewer adoption, which the paper admits is an incentive problem it cannot solve directly.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) -- cited as widely used but repetitive and containing ambiguous/jurisdiction-dependent content
XSTest (Roettger et al. 2024) -- overrefusal dataset, small
StrongREJECT (Souly et al. 2024) -- cited as good practice for aggregating developer guidelines; its Gemma-2B judge used in the case study
CoCoNot (Brahman et al. 2024) -- larger overrefusal dataset with 1,306 XSTest-like examples
OR-Bench (Cui et al. 2025) -- larger overrefusal set but includes ambiguous dual-use prompts
AILuminate (MLCommons 2025) -- cited as a step toward larger safety benchmarks
WildChat (Zhao et al. 2024) -- suggested source for real-world multi-turn prompts
Original large-scale judge-comparison corpus of 5,269,564 model generations
quantitative_results: GCG ASR against Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct varies between 71% and 85% across reasonable implementation variants (Table 1); drops to 47% with no system message; int8 quantization gives 71% (14% lower than an 85% BF16 setup)
Meta chat template + strict token filter (BF16) = 0.77 ASR vs HuggingFace default template + strict filter = 0.85 (8% gap); nanoGCG filter = 0.78; Allow-Non-ASCII = 0.73 (Table 1)
Incorrect whitespace-token handling reduces GCG many-trial ASR by 28% against Llama-2-7B-chat-hf (Figure 2)
Judge disagreement of up to 25% for specific attacks and up to 24% across models, from a study of 5,269,564 generations comparing HarmBench Llama-2-13B vs StrongREJECT Gemma-2B (Figure 3)
Commonly used safety datasets contain only ~100-500 harmful prompts; evaluations are further subsampled to as few as 40, 50, 100, or 159 samples
Binomial/Clopper-Pearson example: n=150, ns=75 gives p standard deviation 0.0408 and 95% CI [0.417, 0.583]
Attack cost illustration: GCG default = 256,000 forward + 500 backward passes per prompt; PAIR = up to 60 generations each from victim/attacker/judge, ~60,000 forward passes per prompt
CoCoNot contains 1,306 examples similar to XSTest prompts
baselines_compared: Attacks discussed/measured: GCG (primary case study), PAIR, AutoDAN, AmpleGCG, BEAST, PGD
Defenses discussed: R2D2 (adversarially trained against GCG; generalizes to AutoDAN but remains susceptible to PAIR), circuit-breaker models
Judges compared: HarmBench Llama-2-13B classifier vs StrongREJECT Gemma-2B judge (plus mention of custom classifiers and GPT/Llama prompt-template judges)
Toolboxes referenced: HarmBench, JailbreakBench, nanoGCG, AdversariaLLM -- as standardization efforts
Note: as a position paper it compares evaluation practices, not a proposed method against baselines
